title: roperators: a gentle tour
text: Let’s start one nearly everyone misses coming languages — gluing strings together +. added : something can’t Python — string division, simply counts many times pattern turns (regular expressions welcome):
code: my_string <- "using infix (%) operators " %+% "lets R do string addition" my_string #> [1] "using infix (%) operators lets R do string addition"  # subtraction removes a pattern my_string %-% "lets R do string addition" #> [1] "using infix (%) operators "  # multiplication repeats (%*% was already taken, so it's %s*%) "ha" %s*% 3 #>       ha  #> "hahaha" "an apple a day keeps the malignant spirit of Steve Jobs at bay" %s/% "a" #> a  #> 8  # with a regular expression "an apple a day keeps the malignant spirit of Steve Jobs at bay" %s/% "Steve Jobs|apple" #> Steve Jobs|apple  #>                2

title: roperators: a gentle tour
text: many times written something like df$x[long$condition] <- df$x[long$condition] + 1? line barely fits page. Let’s make kinder: full set %+=%, %-=%, %*=%, %/=%, %^=%, %root=%, %log=%. %+=% %-=% happy strings, :
code: x <- 1 x %+=% 2 x #> [1] 3  d <- iris # add 1 to setosa sepal lengths, in place d$Sepal.Length[d$Species == "setosa"] %+=% 1 x <- "ab" x %+=% "c" x #> [1] "abc"

what: Filling in missing values and regex matches
title: roperators: a gentle tour
text: %na<-% gently fills NAs, %regex=% / %regex<-% edit place:
code: x <- c(NA, 1, 2, 3) x %na<-% 0 x #> [1] 0 1 2 3  x <- c("a1b", "b1", "c", "d0") x %regex=% c("\\d+", "#")   # replace just the matched part x #> [1] "a#b" "b#"  "c"   "d#"

what: When NA == NA ought to be TRUE
title: roperators: a gentle tour
text: NA doesn’t technically equal another NA — time, ’re actually , ’d like . many statements quietly broken exactly ? %>=% %<=% carry gentle NA-handling.
code: a <- c(NA, "foo", "foo", NA) b <- c(NA, "foo", "bar", "bar")  a == b      # base R: the NA leaks through #> [1]    NA  TRUE FALSE    NA a %==% b    # roperators: NA == NA is treated as TRUE #> [1]  TRUE  TRUE FALSE FALSE

title: roperators: a gentle tour
text: one catches almost everyone, really isn’t fault — ’s just computers hold decimals:

title: roperators: a gentle tour
code: 5 %><% c(1, 10)    # strictly between #> [1] TRUE 1 %>=<% c(1, 10)   # inclusive #> [1] TRUE 5 %><% c(10, 1)    # reversed bounds are fine too — no need to worry about order #> [1] TRUE  # %===% is strict value-AND-class equality, like JavaScript's === x <- int(2) x == 2       # TRUE #> [1] TRUE x %===% 2    # FALSE (different class) #> [1] FALSE x %===% int(2) #> [1] TRUE

title: roperators: a gentle tour
code: "z" %ni% c("a", "b", "c")    # not in #> [1] TRUE TRUE %xor% FALSE             # exclusive or #> [1] TRUE TRUE %aon% TRUE              # all-or-nothing: both TRUE, or both FALSE #> [1] TRUE  # SQL-style LIKE c("FOO", "bar", "fizz") %rlike% "foo"   # case-insensitive #> [1]  TRUE FALSE FALSE c("dOe", "doe")         %perl% "[a-z]O" # case-sensitive, Perl regex #> [1]  TRUE FALSE

title: roperators: a gentle tour
text: new friends, added release. f() — string interpolation (R’s f-strings). Anything inside { } evaluated right call : %else% — calm fallback expression might error (fallback runs ’s actually needed): %/0% — safe division returns NA rather letting Inf NaN wander next sum() mean(): %+-% — tolerance interval drops straight operators: %~% — forgiving string equality ignores case stray whitespace — string cousin %~=%: .percent() — proportions, dressed :
code: who <- "Ben"; n <- 2 f("Hi {who}, you have {n} new message{if (n != 1) 's'}") #> [1] "Hi Ben, you have 2 new messages" f("today's first letters: {head(LETTERS, n)}")   # vectors are tidied up for you #> [1] "today's first letters: A, B" sqrt("not a number") %else% NA_real_ #> [1] NA (1:3)[[99]]          %else% "out of range" #> [1] "out of range" c(10, 20, 30) %/0% c(2, 0, 5) #> [1]  5 NA  6 5 %+-% 0.5 #> [1] 4.5 5.5 4.9 %><% (5 %+-% 0.5) #> [1] TRUE "  Yes " %~% "yes" #> [1] TRUE c("Apple", "PEAR") %~% c("apple", "pear") #> [1] TRUE TRUE as.percent(c(0.1, 0.005, 2 / 3)) #> [1] "10.0%" "0.5%"  "66.7%" as.percent(2 / 3, digits = 0) #> [1] "67%"

title: roperators: a gentle tour
text: R’s conversion syntax touch wordy. trim :
code: chr(42)      # as.character() #> [1] "42" int(42.9)    # as.integer() #> [1] 42 num("4.2")   # as.numeric() #> [1] 4.2 bool("TRUE") # as.logical() #> [1] TRUE  # the famous factor-to-number stumble, smoothed over: fac <- factor(c(11, 22, 33)) as.numeric(fac)     # 1 2 3  -- almost never what you wanted #> [1] 1 2 3 f.as.numeric(fac)   # 11 22 33 #> [1] 11 22 33  # and convert to a class chosen at run time as.class(255, "roman") #> [1] CCLV

title: roperators: a gentle tour
text: Rather chaining five conditions, can ask one calm question: ’s whole family .*_or_null() predicates , lovely checking optional function arguments without fuss.
code: # would any of these break a calculation? is.bad_for_calcs(c(1, NA, Inf, NaN, 5)) #> [1] FALSE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE  is.scalar(1) #> [1] TRUE is.constant(c(1, 1, 1)) #> [1] TRUE is.binary(c("a", "b", "a")) #> [1] TRUE

title: roperators: a gentle tour
code: # pulling pieces out of vectors and strings get_1st_word("Ada Lovelace") #> [1] "Ada" get_last_word("Ada Lovelace") #> [1] "Lovelace" get_most_frequent(c("a", "b", "b", "c", "b")) #> [1] "b"  # Oxford-comma joining, done for you paste_oxford("Tom", "Dick", "Harry") #> [1] "Tom, Dick, and Harry"  # complete-cases stats: just add _cc for na.rm = TRUE mean_cc(c(1, 2, NA)) #> [1] 1.5 sd_cc(c(1, 2, 3, NA)) #> [1] 1  # little environment checks get_os() #> [1] "linux" get_R_version() #> [1] "4.6.0"  # and file-extension checks is_csv_file(c("a.csv", "b.txt")) #> [1]  TRUE FALSE

title: roperators: a gentle tour
text: names shared purpose wider world — %+% ggplot2, %like%-style matching data.table. ’ve got loaded well, just reach namespaced form (roperators::%+%) matters, everyone gets along fine.

what: Additional Operators to Help You Write Cleaner R Code
title: Additional Operators to Help You Write Cleaner R Code
text: Small, friendly operators helpers let R code read way already think . R wonderful language work — asks write something clunkier thought head. roperators smooths moments . Nothing magic underneath: just handful small, well-behaved operators helpers, base R — friendly welcome folks arriving Python elsewhere. (’s pronounced rop-er-ators, r-operators.)
code: "foo" %+% "bar"               # add strings #> [1] "foobar" (0.1 + 0.1 + 0.1) %~=% 0.3    # floating-point equality that works #> [1] TRUE c(1, NA) %==% c(1, NA)        # NA == NA treated as TRUE #> [1] TRUE TRUE

title: Additional Operators to Help You Write Cleaner R Code
code: # released version, from CRAN: install.packages("roperators")  # or the development version, from GitHub: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("BenWiseman/roperators") library(roperators)  "hello " %+% "world"   # your first win #> [1] "hello world"

title: Additional Operators to Help You Write Cleaner R Code
text: package website interactive finder — search ’re trying (“join two strings”, “missing values”, “read file”…) jump straight docs. Either way, full reference lists everything. New ? Take 5-minute guided tour, skim tour features .

title: Additional Operators to Help You Write Cleaner R Code
text: Let’s start one nearly everyone misses coming languages — adding strings together:
code: "using infix (%) operators " %+% "lets R do string addition" #> [1] "using infix (%) operators lets R do string addition"  "abcabc" %-% "c"            # subtract a pattern #> [1] "abab" "ha" %s*% 3                 # multiply #>       ha  #> "hahaha" "an apple a day" %s/% "a"   # divide: how many times does the pattern appear? #> a  #> 4

title: Additional Operators to Help You Write Cleaner R Code
text: ’ve written long line twice just nudge column one. needn’t: ’s whole family — %-=%, %*=%, %/=%, %^=%, %root=%, %log=% — plus %regex=% (apply regex place), %regex<-% (overwrite matching elements), %na<-% (fill NAs):
code: x <- 1 x %+=% 2 x #> [1] 3  d <- iris d$Sepal.Length[d$Species == "setosa"] %+=% 1   # add 1, in place x <- c(1, NA, 3, NA) x %na<-% 0 x #> [1] 1 0 3 0

title: Additional Operators to Help You Write Cleaner R Code
text: NA floating point trip almost everyone — ’s fault, just defaults: also get %===% (strict value--class equality, like JavaScript’s ===), %>=% / %<=% (NA equality), %>~% / %<~% (greater/less approximately equal).
code: c(1, NA, 3) == c(1, NA, 4)    # base R: the NA leaks through #> [1]  TRUE    NA FALSE c(1, NA, 3) %==% c(1, NA, 4)  # roperators: NA == NA is treated as TRUE #> [1]  TRUE  TRUE FALSE  (0.1 + 0.1 + 0.1) == 0.3      # base R: FALSE (floating point) #> [1] FALSE (0.1 + 0.1 + 0.1) %~=% 0.3    # roperators: TRUE #> [1] TRUE  5 %><% c(1, 10)   # strictly between #> [1] TRUE 1 %>=<% c(1, 10)  # between, inclusive #> [1] TRUE

title: Additional Operators to Help You Write Cleaner R Code
text: logical conveniences, plus SQL-style pattern matching:
code: "z" %ni% c("a", "b", "c")          # not in #> [1] TRUE TRUE %xor% FALSE                   # exclusive or #> [1] TRUE c("FOO", "bar") %rlike% "foo"      # case-insensitive LIKE #> [1]  TRUE FALSE

title: Additional Operators to Help You Write Cleaner R Code
text: arrived 1.4 (see changelog):
code: # f-strings: interpolate expressions straight into a string name <- "Ben"; n <- 2 f("Hi {name}, you have {n} message{if (n != 1) 's'}") #> [1] "Hi Ben, you have 2 messages"  # %else%: a fallback when an expression errors sqrt("not a number") %else% NA_real_ #> [1] NA  # %/0%: safe division, so a stray zero never poisons your means 10 %/0% 0 #> [1] NA  # %+-%: a tolerance interval that drops right into the 'between' operators 4.9 %><% (5 %+-% 0.5) #> [1] TRUE  # %~%: forgiving, case/whitespace-insensitive string equality "  Yes " %~% "yes" #> [1] TRUE  # as.percent(): proportions, dressed up as tidy percentages as.percent(c(0.1, 0.005, 2/3)) #> [1] "10.0%" "0.5%"  "66.7%"

title: Additional Operators to Help You Write Cleaner R Code
text: Shorter names conversions reach time: ’s matching set .*_or_null() predicates , along .scalar(), .constant(), .binary(), friends — handy keeping guard clauses short.
code: int("42"); chr(42); num("4.2"); bool("TRUE")   # as.integer(), as.character(), ... #> [1] 42 #> [1] "42" #> [1] 4.2 #> [1] TRUE  # no more as.numeric(as.character(factor_var)): f.as.numeric(factor(c(11, 22, 33))) #> [1] 11 22 33  # TRUE where a value is NA, NaN, or Inf: is.bad_for_calcs(c(1, NA, Inf, NaN)) #> [1] FALSE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE

title: Additional Operators to Help You Write Cleaner R Code
text: …plus OS checks (get_os(), .os_mac(), …), file-extension checks (is_csv_file(), …), read.tsv() / read.psv().
code: get_1st_word("Ada Lovelace")           # and get_last_word / get_nth_word #> [1] "Ada" get_most_frequent(c("a", "b", "b"))    # the mode(s) #> [1] "b" paste_oxford("Tom", "Dick", "Harry")   # Oxford-comma joining #> [1] "Tom, Dick, and Harry"  mean_cc(c(1, 2, NA))                   # *_cc = the base function with na.rm = TRUE #> [1] 1.5

title: Additional Operators to Help You Write Cleaner R Code
text: Take 5-minute guided tour → Browse functions → quick note shared names: %+% also ggplot2’s, %like%-style matching data.table’s. load , use namespaced form (roperators::%+%) matters.

title: Arithmetic convenience operators — arithmetic_sugar
text: %+-% builds tolerance interval: x %+-% y returns c(x - y, x + y), slots straight "" operators comparisons. %/0% safe division returns NA instead Inf/NaN dividing zero, stray zero poison downstream sum mean.

title: Arithmetic convenience operators — arithmetic_sugar
text: x numeric value (vector) y numeric value (vector)

title: Arithmetic convenience operators — arithmetic_sugar
text: %+-% returns length-2 numeric vector   c(lower, upper); %/0% returns x / y   divide--zero results replaced NA.

title: Arithmetic convenience operators — arithmetic_sugar
text: Ben Wiseman, benjamin.h.wiseman@gmail.com

what: Format a proportion as a percentage string — as.percent
title: Format a proportion as a percentage string — as.percent
text: Turn proportion (0.75) human-friendly percentage string ("75%").

title: Format a proportion as a percentage string — as.percent
code: as.percent(x, digits = 1, ...)

title: Format a proportion as a percentage string — as.percent
text: x numeric proportion, vector proportions, 1 represents one hundred percent digits number decimal places show ... arguments passed formatC

title: Format a proportion as a percentage string — as.percent
text: character vector percentage strings.

title: Format a proportion as a percentage string — as.percent
text: Ben Wiseman, benjamin.h.wiseman@gmail.com

title: Format a proportion as a percentage string — as.percent

what: Assign to a vector only where a regular expression matches — %regex<-%
title: Assign to a vector only where a regular expression matches — %regex<-%
text: takes two arguments, just like gsub: pattern replacement. overwrites entire element wherever pattern matches. want substitute matched portion, use %regex=% instead; replace matches nothing (""), use %-% %-=%.

title: Assign to a vector only where a regular expression matches — %regex<-%
code: x %regex<-% value

title: Assign to a vector only where a regular expression matches — %regex<-%
text: x character vector value length-2 character vector form c(pattern, replacement)

title: Assign to a vector only where a regular expression matches — %regex<-%
text: Used side effect reassigning x calling   environment; returns modified x invisibly.

title: Assign to a vector only where a regular expression matches — %regex<-%
text: Ben Wiseman, benjamin.h.wiseman@gmail.com

title: Assign to a vector only where a regular expression matches — %regex<-%
code: x <- c("a1b", "b1", "c", "d0")  # overwrite any element containing a digit x %regex<-% c("\\d+", "x") print(x) #> [1] "x" "x" "c" "x" # "x" "b" "c" "x"

title: Assignment (in-place modifier) operators — assign_ops
text: Modify stored value left-hand-side object place. equivalent operators +=, -=, *=, /= languages like C++ 'Python'. %+=% %-=% also work strings, %-=% accepts regular expressions.

title: Assignment (in-place modifier) operators — assign_ops
code: x %+=% y  x %-=% y  x %*=% y  x %/=% y  x %^=% y  x %log=% y  x %root=% y

title: Assignment (in-place modifier) operators — assign_ops
text: x stored value y value modify stored value

title: Assignment (in-place modifier) operators — assign_ops
text: Used side effect reassigning x calling   environment; returns new value x invisibly.

title: Assignment (in-place modifier) operators — assign_ops
text: Ben Wiseman, benjamin.h.wiseman@gmail.com

title: Assignment (in-place modifier) operators — assign_ops
code: x <- 1 x %+=% 2 x == 3 # TRUE #> [1] TRUE  x %-=% 3 x == 0 # TRUE #> [1] TRUE  # Or with data frames... test <- iris  # Simply modify in place test$Sepal.Length[test$Species == 'setosa' & test$Petal.Length < 1.5] %+=% 1  # Which is much nicer than typing: test$Sepal.Length[test$Species == 'setosa' & test$Petal.Length < 1.5] <- test$Sepal.Length[test$Species == 'setosa' & test$Petal.Length < 1.5] + 1 # ...which is over the 100 character limit for R documentation!  # %+=% and %-=% also work with strings x <- "ab" x %+=% "c" x %-=% "b" x == "ac" # TRUE #> [1] TRUE  # %-=% can also take regular expressions x <- "foobar" x %-=% "[fb]" print(x) #> [1] "ooar" # "ooar"

title: Choose and permute operators — choose_permute
text: Shorthand infix operators common combinatorics: n %C% k gives number combinations ("n choose k"), n %P% k gives number permutations ("n permute k").

title: Choose and permute operators — choose_permute
code: n %C% k  n %P% k

title: Choose and permute operators — choose_permute
text: n whole number (n "n choose/permute k") k whole number (k "n choose/permute k")

title: Choose and permute operators — choose_permute
text: numeric value.

title: Choose and permute operators — choose_permute
text: Ben Wiseman, benjamin.h.wiseman@gmail.com

title: Cleaner type-conversion functions — chr
text: Short aliases common .* conversions. nothing magical , shorter names can make data-wrangling code much easier read, especially users coming languages. .class() additionally converts class chosen name run time.

title: Cleaner type-conversion functions — chr
code: chr(x, ...)  int(x, ...)  dbl(x, ...)  num(x, ...)  bool(x, ...)  as.class(x, class)

title: Cleaner type-conversion functions — chr
text: x value converted ... arguments passed underlying .* function class character; name class convert x (used .class)

title: Cleaner type-conversion functions — chr
text: value x coerced requested type.

title: Cleaner type-conversion functions — chr
text: Steven Nydick, steven.nydick@kornferry.com Ben Wiseman, benjamin.h.wiseman@gmail.com

title: Cleaner type-conversion functions — chr
code: chr(42)    # "42" = as.character() #> [1] "42" int(42.1)  # 42L  = as.integer() #> [1] 42 dbl("42")  # 42   = as.double() #> [1] 42 num("42")  # 42   = as.numeric() #> [1] 42 bool(42)   # TRUE = as.logical() #> [1] TRUE   # as.class() converts to an arbitrary class chosen by name: as.class(255, "roman") # CCLV #> [1] CCLV

what: Comparison operators with better missing-value handling — comparisons
title: Comparison operators with better missing-value handling — comparisons
text: set comparison operators improve base R treating missing values comparable (two NAs considered equal) adding convenient interval ("") tests. operators : %==% - equality treats NA == NA TRUE. %===% - strict equality value class,     familiar 'JavaScript' ===. %>=%, %<=% - greater/less equal ,     missing-value equality. %><%, %>=<% - , ends excluded     included respectively. approximate (floating-point) comparisons, see floating_point_comparisons.

title: Comparison operators with better missing-value handling — comparisons
Comparison operators with better missing-value handling — comparisons
text: x vector y vector ("" operators, length-2 vector form c(lower, upper))

title: Comparison operators with better missing-value handling — comparisons
text: logical vector.

title: Comparison operators with better missing-value handling — comparisons
text: Ben Wiseman, benjamin.h.wiseman@gmail.com

title: Comparison operators with better missing-value handling — comparisons
code: ## Equality and ordering, with missing-value equality  c(1, NA, 3, 4)  ==  c(1, NA, 4, 3) #> [1]  TRUE    NA FALSE FALSE  #  TRUE    NA  FALSE FALSE   c(1, NA, 3, 4) %==% c(1, NA, 4, 3) #> [1]  TRUE  TRUE FALSE FALSE  #  TRUE  TRUE  FALSE FALSE   c(1, NA, 3, 4) %>=% c(1, NA, 4, 3) #> [1]  TRUE  TRUE FALSE  TRUE  #  TRUE  TRUE FALSE  TRUE   c(1, NA, 3, 4) %<=% c(1, NA, 4, 3) #> [1]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE  #  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE   ## Strict equality - a la 'JavaScript' ===  # Only TRUE if the class AND value of x and y are the same  x <- int(2)  y <- 2  x == y         # TRUE #> [1] TRUE  x %===% y      # FALSE #> [1] FALSE  x %===% int(y) # TRUE #> [1] TRUE   ## Between  # ends excluded  2 %><% c(1, 3)  # TRUE #> [1] TRUE  3 %><% c(1, 3)  # FALSE #> [1] FALSE   # ends included  2 %>=<% c(1, 3) # TRUE #> [1] TRUE  3 %>=<% c(1, 3) # TRUE #> [1] TRUE

what: Statistics/Summaries with (Only) Missing Data Removed — complete_cases
title: Statistics/Summaries with (Only) Missing Data Removed — complete_cases
text: Univariate bivariate summaries statistics least missing data removed (complete-cases correlations). typically default arguments standard statistics functions.

title: Statistics/Summaries with (Only) Missing Data Removed — complete_cases
code: length_cc(x, ...)  n_unique_cc(x, ...)  min_cc(x, ...)  max_cc(x, ...)  range_cc(x, ...)  all_cc(x, ...)  any_cc(x, ...)  sum_cc(x, ...)  prod_cc(x, ...)  mean_cc(x, ...)  median_cc(x, ...)  var_cc(x, y = NULL, ...)  cov_cc(x, y = NULL, ...)  cor_cc(x, y = NULL, ...)  sd_cc(x, ...)  weighted.mean_cc(x, w, ...)  quantile_cc(x, ...)  IQR_cc(x, ...)  mad_cc(x, ...)  rowSums_cc(x, ...)  colSums_cc(x, ...)  rowMeans_cc(x, ..., rescale = FALSE)  colMeans_cc(x, ..., rescale = FALSE)

title: Statistics/Summaries with (Only) Missing Data Removed — complete_cases
text: x R object.  Currently methods     numeric/logical vectors date,     date-time time interval objects.  Complex vectors     allowed trim = 0, . ... arguments pass wrapped functions y NULL (default) vector, matrix data frame     compatible dimensions x.   default equivalent     y = x (efficient). w numerical vector weights length x giving     weights use elements x. rescale whether rescale matrix/df/vector calculating summaries

title: Statistics/Summaries with (Only) Missing Data Removed — complete_cases
text: value base/stats function one wraps (  example numeric summary, vector, matrix), computed missing   values removed default.

title: Statistics/Summaries with (Only) Missing Data Removed — complete_cases
code: n_o <- 20 n_m <- round(n_o / 3) x   <- rnorm(n_o) y   <- rnorm(n_o)  x[sample(n_o, n_m)] <- NA y[sample(n_o, n_m)] <- NA  mean_cc(x)   # mean of complete cases #> [1] -0.2644023 mean_cc(y) #> [1] 0.1887549 var_cc(x)    # variance of complete cases #> [1] 1.439278 var_cc(y) #> [1] 1.09564 cor_cc(x, y) # correlation between available cases #> [1] 0.2796028  # the row/column helpers also drop NAs by default m <- matrix(c(1, NA, 3, 4, 5, 9), nrow = 2) rowMeans_cc(m) #> [1] 3.0 6.5 colSums_cc(m) #> [1]  1  7 14  # colMeans_cc()/rowMeans_cc() can z-score each column first via rescale = TRUE colMeans_cc(matrix(1:6, nrow = 3), rescale = TRUE) #> [1] 0 0

title: Vector content checks — content_checks
text: Quick checks vector contains. .constant() TRUE x holds one unique value (ignoring NA), .binary() TRUE holds two.

title: Vector content checks — content_checks
code: is.constant(x)  is.binary(x)

title: Vector content checks — content_checks
text: x object tested

title: Vector content checks — content_checks
text: logical value.

title: Vector content checks — content_checks
code: is.constant(c(1, 1, 1))     # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is.constant(c(1, 2, 1))     # FALSE #> [1] FALSE is.binary(c("a", "b", NA))  # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is.binary(c("a", "b", "c")) # FALSE #> [1] FALSE

title: String interpolation (f-strings for R) — f
text: Interpolate R expressions string, like 'Python' f-strings. Anything inside curly braces evaluated calling environment inserted string. Doubled braces treated single literal brace.

title: String interpolation (f-strings for R) — f
code: f(..., .envir = parent.frame())

title: String interpolation (f-strings for R) — f
text: ... one strings containing {expr} placeholders .envir environment evaluate placeholders (defaults calling environment)

title: String interpolation (f-strings for R) — f
text: character vector length input, every   {expr} replaced evaluated, comma-collapsed value.

title: String interpolation (f-strings for R) — f
text: f also popular name throwaway functions, aware may mask (masked ) local f .

title: String interpolation (f-strings for R) — f
text: Ben Wiseman, benjamin.h.wiseman@gmail.com

title: String interpolation (f-strings for R) — f
code: name <- "Ben" n    <- 2 f("Hi {name}, you have {n} new messages") #> [1] "Hi Ben, you have 2 new messages" f("{n} + {n} = {n + n}") #> [1] "2 + 2 = 4"  # vectors are collapsed with ", " f("today's letters: {head(LETTERS, n)}") #> [1] "today's letters: A, B"

what: Convert a factor with numeric labels into a numeric vector — f.as.numeric
title: Convert a factor with numeric labels into a numeric vector — f.as.numeric
text: Converting factor .numeric() returns underlying integer codes, labels, rarely want labels numbers. f..numeric() returns labels numbers instead.

title: Convert a factor with numeric labels into a numeric vector — f.as.numeric
code: f.as.numeric(x)

title: Convert a factor with numeric labels into a numeric vector — f.as.numeric
text: x factor numeric labels

title: Convert a factor with numeric labels into a numeric vector — f.as.numeric
text: numeric vector factor's labels.

title: Convert a factor with numeric labels into a numeric vector — f.as.numeric
text: Ulrike Grömping, groemping@beuth-hochschule.de Ben Wiseman, benjamin.h.wiseman@gmail.com

title: Convert a factor with numeric labels into a numeric vector — f.as.numeric
code: x <- factor(c(11, 22, 33, 99))  as.numeric(x) #> [1] 1 2 3 4 # 1 2 3 4      # the integer codes - NOT usually what you want  f.as.numeric(x) #> [1] 11 22 33 99 # 11 22 33 99  # the labels as numbers - usually what you want  # equivalent to the clunkier base idiom: as.numeric(as.character(x)) #> [1] 11 22 33 99

title: Floating-point comparison operators — floating_point_comparisons
text: important set operators missing base R. Using == two non-integer numbers can give unexpected results (see examples), way floating-point numbers represented. operators instead test equality small tolerance, via .equal. fuller explanation, see https://docs.oracle.com/cd/E19957-01/806-3568/ncg_goldberg.html.

title: Floating-point comparison operators — floating_point_comparisons
text: x numeric y numeric

title: Floating-point comparison operators — floating_point_comparisons
text: logical value.

title: Floating-point comparison operators — floating_point_comparisons
text: Ben Wiseman, benjamin.h.wiseman@gmail.com

title: Floating-point comparison operators — floating_point_comparisons
code: ## Floating-point test of equality  # base R:  (0.1 + 0.1 + 0.1) == 0.3   # FALSE #> [1] FALSE  # with roperators:  (0.1 + 0.1 + 0.1) %~=% 0.3 # TRUE #> [1] TRUE   # Note how the base >= and <= behave here:  (0.1 + 0.1 + 0.1) %>=% 0.3 # TRUE #> [1] TRUE  (0.1 + 0.1 + 0.1) %<=% 0.3 # FALSE #> [1] FALSE   # Use %>~% and %<~% for greater/less than OR approximately equal  (0.1 + 0.1 + 0.1) %>~% 0.3 # TRUE #> [1] TRUE  (0.1 + 0.1 + 0.1) %<~% 0.3 # TRUE #> [1] TRUE

title: Fuzzy (case- and whitespace-insensitive) string equality — %~%
text: string counterpart numeric operator %~=% (see floating_point_comparisons). x %~% y TRUE x y equal ignoring case, leading/trailing whitespace, runs internal whitespace - handy joining matching messy, hand-entered data.

title: Fuzzy (case- and whitespace-insensitive) string equality — %~%
code: x %~% y

title: Fuzzy (case- and whitespace-insensitive) string equality — %~%
text: x character vector y character vector

title: Fuzzy (case- and whitespace-insensitive) string equality — %~%
text: logical vector, NA either side NA.

title: Fuzzy (case- and whitespace-insensitive) string equality — %~%
text: Ben Wiseman, benjamin.h.wiseman@gmail.com

title: Fuzzy (case- and whitespace-insensitive) string equality — %~%
code: "Foo " %~% "foo"                   # TRUE #> [1] TRUE "a  b" %~% "a b"                    # TRUE #> [1] TRUE c("Yes", "NO") %~% c("yes", "no")  # TRUE TRUE #> [1] TRUE TRUE

what: Inline fallback for expressions that might error — %else%
title: Inline fallback for expressions that might error — %else%
text: Evaluate left-hand side; raises error, return right-hand side instead. right-hand side evaluated needed (lazily), expensive side-effecting fallback safe use.

title: Inline fallback for expressions that might error — %else%
code: expr %else% alternative

title: Inline fallback for expressions that might error — %else%
text: expr expression try alternative value (expression) return expr errors

title: Inline fallback for expressions that might error — %else%
text: value expr, alternative expr   raised error.

title: Inline fallback for expressions that might error — %else%
text: Ben Wiseman, benjamin.h.wiseman@gmail.com

title: Inline fallback for expressions that might error — %else%
code: sqrt(4)         %else% NA_real_     # 2 #> [1] 2 sqrt("a")       %else% NA_real_     # NA, instead of an error #> [1] NA (1:3)[[99]]     %else% "out of range" #> [1] "out of range" stop("boom")    %else% "recovered" #> [1] "recovered"

title: Inline integration operator — integrate
text: inline call integrate returns value integral directly, rather usual list.

title: Inline integration operator — integrate
code: f %integrate% range

title: Inline integration operator — integrate
text: f function numeric return value range length-2 numeric vector, c(lower, upper)

title: Inline integration operator — integrate
text: single numeric value: value integral.

title: Inline integration operator — integrate
text: Ben Wiseman, benjamin.h.wiseman@gmail.com

title: Inline integration operator — integrate
code: f <- function(x) x^2 f %integrate% c(0, 1) # 0.3333333 #> [1] 0.3333333  # compared with base R, which returns a list: str(integrate(f, 0, 1)) #> List of 5 #>  $ value       : num 0.333 #>  $ abs.error   : num 3.7e-15 #>  $ subdivisions: int 1 #>  $ message     : chr "OK" #>  $ call        : language integrate(f = f, lower = 0, upper = 1) #>  - attr(*, "class")= chr "integrate"

title: Logical operators — logicals

what: Operating system and environment checks — os
title: Operating system and environment checks — os
text: Determine current operating system R environment, provide simple flags common questions "Mac?", "64-bit R?", "running inside RStudio?". useful writing code must behave differently across platforms (example, choosing parallel back-end Unix versus Windows).

title: Operating system and environment checks — os
code: get_os()  get_R_version()  get_R_version_age(units = c("years", "months", "weeks", "days"), rounding = 2)  get_latest_CRAN_version()  get_system_python()  is.os_mac()  is.os_win()  is.os_lnx()  is.os_unx()  is.os_x64()  is.os_arm()  is.R_x64()  is.R_revo()  is.RStudio()  is.http_available()

title: Operating system and environment checks — os
text: units character; unit report R version age , one "years", "months", "weeks", "days". rounding integer; number decimal places round age .

title: Operating system and environment checks — os
text: .* checks, single logical value. get_os() returns character string ("win", "mac", "linux", "unix"); get_R_version() get_latest_CRAN_version() return version strings; get_R_version_age() returns numeric age.

title: Operating system and environment checks — os
text: Ben Wiseman, benjamin.h.wiseman@gmail.com Steven Nydick, steven.nydick@kornferry.com

title: Operating system and environment checks — os
code: # determine the operating system get_os() #> [1] "linux"  # test for a particular operating system is.os_mac() #> [1] FALSE is.os_win() #> [1] FALSE is.os_lnx() #> [1] TRUE is.os_unx() #> [1] TRUE  # environment checks is.os_x64() #> [1] TRUE is.RStudio() #> [1] FALSE get_R_version() #> [1] "4.6.0"

what: Modify an object in place by regular expression — %regex=%
title: Modify an object in place by regular expression — %regex=%
text: takes two arguments, just like gsub: pattern replacement. overwrites matched portion element x. want overwrite whole elements match (rather just matched portion), use %regex<-% instead.

title: Modify an object in place by regular expression — %regex=%
code: x %regex=% value

title: Modify an object in place by regular expression — %regex=%
text: x character vector value length-2 character vector form c(pattern, replacement)

title: Modify an object in place by regular expression — %regex=%
text: Used side effect reassigning x calling   environment; returns modified x invisibly.

title: Modify an object in place by regular expression — %regex=%
text: Ben Wiseman, benjamin.h.wiseman@gmail.com

title: Modify an object in place by regular expression — %regex=%
code: x <- c("a1b", "b1", "c", "d0")  # change any digit to "x" x %regex=% c("\\d+", "x") print(x) #> [1] "axb" "bx"  "c"   "dx"  # "axb" "b" "c" "dx"

what: Assign a value to a vector's missing values — %na<-%
title: Assign a value to a vector's missing values — %na<-%
text: %na<-% shortcut assign value NA elements x. replacement may single value (recycled every NA), vector one entry per missing element (filled order), vector length x (values missing positions used).

title: Assign a value to a vector's missing values — %na<-%
code: x %na<-% value

title: Assign a value to a vector's missing values — %na<-%
text: x vector value single value, vector one entry per NA, vector length x

title: Assign a value to a vector's missing values — %na<-%
text: Used side effect reassigning x calling   environment; returns modified x invisibly.

title: Assign a value to a vector's missing values — %na<-%
text: Ben Wiseman, benjamin.h.wiseman@gmail.com

title: Assign a value to a vector's missing values — %na<-%
code: x <- c("a", NA, "c") x %na<-% "b" print(x) #> [1] "a" "b" "c" # "a" "b" "c"  x <- c(1, NA, 3, NA) x %na<-% c(2, 4)   # one replacement per NA, in order print(x) #> [1] 1 2 3 4 # 1 2 3 4

title: Paste and cat helpers — paste_and_cat
text: small family paste/cat conveniences: paste_() - like paste0(), separates     underscore. cat0() - like paste0(), cat (    separator). catN() - like cat0(), appends new line. paste_series() - paste series items together     conjunction, e.g. ", b, c". paste_oxford() - shortcut paste_series() using     Oxford comma.

title: Paste and cat helpers — paste_and_cat
code: paste_(..., collapse = NULL)  cat0(..., file = "", fill = FALSE, labels = NULL, append = FALSE)  catN(..., file = "", fill = FALSE, labels = NULL, append = FALSE)  paste_series(   ...,   sep = c(",", ";"),   conjunction = c("and", "or", "&"),   use_oxford_comma = TRUE )  paste_oxford(...)

title: Paste and cat helpers — paste_and_cat
text: ... one R objects, converted character vectors. collapse optional character string separate results.      NA_character_.  collapse string,     result always string (character length 1). file character - connection, character string naming file print . "" (default), cat prints standard output connection, console unless redirected sink. fill logical (positive) numeric controlling output broken successive lines. see `?cat` labels character vector labels lines printed. Ignored fill FALSE. append logical. used argument file     name file (connection "|cmd").     TRUE output appended     file; otherwise, overwrite contents     file. sep character vector strings append element conjunction conjunction used join final elements series, "", "", "&" use_oxford_comma logical; whether use Oxford comma (standard American English) conjunction

title: Paste and cat helpers — paste_and_cat
text: paste_(), paste_series(), paste_oxford()   return character vector. cat0() catN() called   side effect (printing) return NULL invisibly.

title: Paste and cat helpers — paste_and_cat
text: Steven Nydick, steven.nydick@kornferry.com

title: Paste and cat helpers — paste_and_cat
code: paste_("a", "b", "c")          # "a_b_c"  (paste0 with an underscore) #> [1] "a_b_c" cat0("no", "spaces", "here")   # prints: nospaceshere #> nospaceshere catN("...and this one", " ends with a newline") #> ...and this one ends with a newline   paste_series("a") #> [1] "a" paste_series("a", "b") #> [1] "a and b" paste_series("a", "b", "c") #> [1] "a, b, and c" # works if putting entries into c function paste_series(c("a", "b", "c"), "d") #> [1] "a, b, c, and d" # can use oxford comma or not paste_series("a", "b", "c",              use_oxford_comma = TRUE) #> [1] "a, b, and c" paste_series("a", "b", "c",              use_oxford_comma = FALSE) #> [1] "a, b and c" # makes no difference if fewer than 3 items paste_series("a", "b",              use_oxford_comma = TRUE) #> [1] "a and b"

title: SQL-style pattern-matching operators — pattern_matching
text: Convenience operators regular-expression matching, inspired SQL's LIKE. takes character vector x single pattern, returns logical vector length x. %rlike% matches case-insensitively, equivalent grepl(pattern, x, ignore.case = TRUE). %perl% matches case-sensitively using Perl-compatible regular expressions, equivalent grepl(pattern, x, perl = TRUE).

title: SQL-style pattern-matching operators — pattern_matching
code: x %rlike% pattern  x %perl% pattern

title: SQL-style pattern-matching operators — pattern_matching
text: x character vector pattern single character expression (regular expression)

title: SQL-style pattern-matching operators — pattern_matching
text: logical vector length x.

title: SQL-style pattern-matching operators — pattern_matching
text: working data.table, prefer (faster) %like% operator.

title: SQL-style pattern-matching operators — pattern_matching
text: Ben Wiseman, benjamin.h.wiseman@gmail.com

title: SQL-style pattern-matching operators — pattern_matching
code: x <- c("foo", "bar", "dOe", "rei", "mei", "obo")  # case-insensitive: where x contains an "o" (any case) x[x %rlike% "O"] #> [1] "foo" "dOe" "obo" # [1] "foo" "dOe" "obo"  # case-sensitive Perl matching: middle letter is an upper-case "O" x[x %perl% "[a-z]O[a-z]"] #> [1] "dOe" # [1] "dOe"

code: seq_around(origin = 1, n = 1, spacing = 0.25)

text: origin number centre sequence n number points create (single whole number) spacing distance two neighbouring points

title: Sequence of evenly spaced points around an origin — seq_around
text: numeric vector. Defaults 1 called arguments,   mirror default behaviour seq.

String arithmetic operators — string_arithmetic
text: Perform string concatenation arithmetic similar way languages. String addition (%+%) glues strings together, string subtraction (%-%) removes pattern, string multiplication (%s*%) repeats string, string division (%s/%) counts many times pattern occurs. String division equivalent languages like 'Python', yet arguably useful string multiplication, accepts regular expressions.

title: String arithmetic operators — string_arithmetic
code: x %+% y  x %-% y  x %s*% y  x %s/% y

title: String arithmetic operators — string_arithmetic
text: x string (character vector) y string (character vector regular expression)

title: String arithmetic operators — string_arithmetic
text: character vector %+%, %-%, %s*%,   integer count %s/%.

title: String arithmetic operators — string_arithmetic
text: Ben Wiseman, benjamin.h.wiseman@gmail.com

title: String arithmetic operators — string_arithmetic
code: ("ab" %+% "c") == "abc" # TRUE #> [1] TRUE ("abc" %-% "b") == "ac" # TRUE #> [1] TRUE ("ac" %s*% 2) == "acac" # TRUE #>   ac  #> TRUE  ("acac" %s/% "c") == 2  # TRUE #>    c  #> TRUE  # String division with a regular expression: "an apple a day keeps the malignant spirit of Steve Jobs at bay" %s/% "Steve Jobs|apple" #> Steve Jobs|apple  #>                2

what: Get the first, last, n-th, or most frequent element or word — get_1st
title: Get the first, last, n-th, or most frequent element or word — get_1st
text: Small helpers pulling pieces vectors strings - handy inside apply-style calls. get_1st(), get_last(), get_nth() extract elements vector; *_word variants split strings words first; get_most_frequent() / get_most_frequent_word() return common value(s).

title: Get the first, last, n-th, or most frequent element or word — get_1st
code: get_1st(x, type = "v")  get_last(x, type = "v")  get_nth(x, n = 1, type = "v")  get_1st_word(x, type = "v", split = " ")  get_last_word(x, type = "v", split = " ")  get_nth_word(x, n = 1, type = "v", split = " ")  get_most_frequent(x, collapse = NULL)  get_most_frequent_word(   x,   ignore.punct = TRUE,   ignore.case = TRUE,   split = " ",   collapse = NULL,   punct.regex = "[[:punct:]]",   punct.replace = "" )

title: Get the first, last, n-th, or most frequent element or word — get_1st
text: x R object, usually vector character string type 'v' (default) index vector (x[1]), 'l' index list (x[[1]]) n integer; n-th element word select split character used separate words (default ' ') collapse optional character; supplied, result pasted single string using separator ignore.punct logical; ignore punctuation marks ignore.case logical; ignore case (TRUE, result lower-case) punct.regex character; regex used strip punctuation (default [[:punct:]]) punct.replace character; replace punctuation (default "")

title: Get the first, last, n-th, or most frequent element or word — get_1st
text: selected element(s) word(s). get_most_frequent*()   return common value(s), character vector unless x   numeric collapse used.

title: Get the first, last, n-th, or most frequent element or word — get_1st
text: Ben Wiseman, benjamin.h.wiseman@gmail.com

title: Get the first, last, n-th, or most frequent element or word — get_1st
code: # a list of split-up car names car_names <- strsplit(row.names(mtcars)[1:5], " ")  sapply(car_names, get_1st) #> [1] "Mazda"  "Mazda"  "Datsun" "Hornet" "Hornet" # [1] "Mazda" "Mazda" "Datsun" "Hornet" "Hornet"  sapply(car_names, get_nth, 2) #> [1] "RX4"        "RX4"        "710"        "4"          "Sportabout" # [1] "RX4" "RX4" "710" "4" "Sportabout"  # Or pull a simple string apart (e.g. someone's full name): get_1st_word(rownames(mtcars)[1:5]) #> [1] "Mazda"  "Mazda"  "Datsun" "Hornet" "Hornet" # [1] "Mazda" "Mazda" "Datsun" "Hornet" "Hornet"  get_last_word(rownames(mtcars)[1:5]) #> [1] "RX4"        "Wag"        "710"        "Drive"      "Sportabout" # [1] "RX4" "Wag" "710" "Drive" "Sportabout"  get_nth_word(rownames(mtcars)[1:5], 2) #> [1] "RX4"        "RX4"        "710"        "4"          "Sportabout" # [1] "RX4" "RX4" "710" "4" "Sportabout"   # get_most_frequent() returns the mode(s) my_stuff <- c(1:10, 10, 5) get_1st(my_stuff)           # 1 #> [1] 1 get_nth(my_stuff, 3)        # 3 #> [1] 3 get_last(my_stuff)          # 5 #> [1] 5 get_most_frequent(my_stuff) # the modes (5 and 10), as a numeric vector #> [1] 10  5  my_chars <- c("a", "b", "b", "a", "g", "o", "l", "d") get_most_frequent(my_chars)                    # "a" "b" #> [1] "a" "b" get_most_frequent(my_chars, collapse = " & ")  # "a & b" #> [1] "a & b"  # the *_word helpers split a string into words first generic_string <- "Who's A good boy? Winston's a good boy!" get_1st_word(generic_string)    # "Who's" #> [1] "Who's" get_nth_word(generic_string, 3) # "good" #> [1] "good" get_last_word(generic_string)   # "boy!" #> [1] "boy!"  # default ignores case and punctuation get_most_frequent_word(generic_string) #> [1] "a"    "boy"  "good" # keep case and punctuation: get_most_frequent_word(generic_string, ignore.case = FALSE, ignore.punct = FALSE) #> [1] "good"

title: Type checks — type_checks
text: collection convenience checks type contents object. returns logical value (vector), keeps guard clauses statements short readable. *_or_null variants handy validating optional arguments, *_for_calcs / *_for_indexing helpers flag values break arithmetic subsetting (NA, NaN, Inf).

title: Type checks — type_checks
code: is.scalar(x)  is.scalar_or_null(x)  is.numeric_or_null(x)  is.character_or_null(x)  is.logical_or_null(x)  is.df_or_null(x)  is.list_or_null(x)  is.atomic_nan(x)  is.irregular_list(x)  any_bad_for_calcs(x, ..., na.rm = FALSE)  all_good_for_calcs(x, ..., na.rm = FALSE)  is.bad_for_indexing(x)  is.good_for_indexing(x)  is.bad_and_equal(x, y)  is.bad_for_calcs(x, na.rm = FALSE)  is.good_for_calcs(x, na.rm = FALSE)  is.null_or_na(x)

title: Type checks — type_checks
text: x object tested ... values tested na.rm TRUE, NA values considered bad calculations y object tested

title: Type checks — type_checks
text: logical value (vector) indicating whether x meets   test.

title: Type checks — type_checks
text: Steven Nydick, steven.nydick@kornferry.com

title: Type checks — type_checks
code: is.scalar(1)              # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is.scalar(c(1, 2))        # FALSE #> [1] FALSE is.numeric_or_null(NULL)  # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is.bad_for_calcs(NA)      # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is.good_for_calcs(1)      # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is.bad_and_equal(NA, NA)  # TRUE #> [1] TRUE

title: roperators 1.4.0
text: f() — string interpolation (“f-strings”): f("Hi {name}, {n} messages"). %else% — inline fallback expression might error: read.csv(path) %else% data.frame(). %+-% — tolerance-interval constructor composes operators: x %><% (5 %+-% 0.5). %/0% — safe division returns NA instead Inf/NaN divide--zero. %~% — case- whitespace-insensitive string equality (string counterpart %~=%). .percent() — format proportion percentage string.

title: roperators 1.4.0
text: .bad_for_calcs() now correctly flags Inf/-Inf bad calculations. Previously infinities slipped , also affected comparison operators (%==%, %>=%, …) rely . .R_x64() now calls .os_unx() correctly (previously referenced function without calling ). seq_around() now accepts ordinary numeric values n; previously errored unless n given explicit integer (e.g. 5L). operators (%><%, %>=<%) now handle reversed bounds, 5 %><% c(10, 1) behaves 5 %><% c(1, 10). %na<-% now also accepts replacement vector one entry per missing value (filled order), addition scalar full-length vector. Removed stray duplicate (subtly broken) internal definition .bad_for_calcs().

title: roperators 1.4.0
text: .class() now vector-safe longer builds parses code string internally. .http_available() uses requireNamespace() instead much slower installed.packages(). get_system_python(), is_rda_file(), is_rds_file() now exported (documented earlier versions never actually exported).

title: roperators 1.4.0
text: Documentation overhaul clarity consistency: tightened every help page, fixed typos throughout, added missing \value documentation runnable examples. %rlike% %perl% now help page (“SQL-style pattern matching”) instead grouped logical operators. Rewritten, comprehensive vignette README.Rmd README’s output always real. Added full testthat test suite covering operators helpers, BugReports URL, pkgdown site configuration, GitHub Actions R-CMD-check pkgdown deployment.

title: roperators 1.3.14
text: CRAN release: 2023-07-20 added .os_arm() check running ARM cpu added get_R_version() pull current R version added get_R_version_age() find old installed R version added get_latest_CRAN_version() find latest version CRAN (rvest required) added get_system_python() return default python called system calls “python” added %C% choose operator %P% permute added %integrate% inline integrations added .http_available() check whether can send HTTP request (via httr, RCurl, system call curl).

title: roperators 1.3.0
text: added seq_around() evenly spaced sequences around origin point Cleaned roxygen comments, DESCRIPTION, NAMESPACE files. Added NEWS.md file track changes package. New complete cases functions (length_cc(), min_cc(), max_cc(), range_cc(), all_cc(), any_cc(), sum_cc(), prod_cc(), mean_cc(), median_cc(), var_cc(), cov_cc(), cor_cc(), sd_cc(), weighted.mean_cc(), quantile_cc(), IQR_cc(), mad_cc(), rowSums_cc(), colSums_cc(), rowMeans_cc(), colMeans_cc()) set default argument standard base/stats functions calculating complete cases. New get_os(), .os_mac, .os_win, .os_lnx, .os_unx helper functions quickly determine operating system. New is_txt_file(), is_csv_file(), is_excel_file(), is_r_file(), is_rdata_file(), is_rda_file(), is_spss_file(), check_ext_against() functions quickly determine file specific file extensions. New .R_Revo() .RStudio() functions determine whether Revolution R RStudio installed. New n_unique() function count unique items n_unique_cc() version complete cases. New .constant() .binary() functions indicate whether vector constant (contains ONE value type, ignoring NA) binary (contains TWO value types, ignoring NA). New get_most_frequent() function get frequent thing(s) x. New get_most_frequent_word() function get frequent word string. New get_nth_word() function get nth word string New .class() pipe-able function arbitrary class conversions New paste_()paste0 uses underscore separate New cat0() analogous paste0 cat New catN() cat0 automatically inserts new line cat New paste_series() paste series things conjunction New paste_oxford() shortcut paste_series oxford comma
